perturbation.original_text: 4.3 Distributor may defer Product shipment for up to [*****] from the original shipping date scheduled by Cisco, provided written or electronic notice (issued, in either case, by an authorized representative of Distributor) is received by Cisco at least [*****] days before the originally scheduled shipping date. Cancelled Purchase Orders, rescheduled shipments or Product configuration changes requested by Distributor less than [*****] days before the original scheduled shipping date shall be subject to (a) acceptance by Cisco, and (b) a charge of [*****] of the total invoice amount relating to the affected Products. Cisco reserves the right to reschedule shipment in cases of configuration changes requested by Distributor within [*****] days of scheduled shipment. No cancellation shall be accepted by Cisco where Products are purchased with implementation Services, including design, customization or installation Services, except as may be set forth in the agreement or Statement of Work under which the Services are to be rendered.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.3 Distributor may defer Product shipment for up to [*****] from the original shipping date scheduled by Cisco, provided written or electronic notice (issued, in either case, by an authorized representative of Distributor) is received by Cisco at least 1 day before the originally scheduled shipping date. Cancelled Purchase Orders, rescheduled shipments or Product configuration changes requested by Distributor less than 1 day before the original scheduled shipping date shall be subject to (a) acceptance by Cisco, and (b) a charge of [*****] of the total invoice amount relating to the affected Products. Cisco reserves the right to reschedule shipment in cases of configuration changes requested by Distributor within 1 day of scheduled shipment. No cancellation shall be accepted by Cisco where Products are purchased with implementation Services, including design, customization or installation Services, except as may be set forth in the agreement or Statement of Work under which the Services are to be rendered.
perturbation.explanation: The change reduces the allowed notice period for deferring or changing product shipments to 1 day, which may conflict with requirements for reasonable notice or cause undue hardship, potentially violating good faith and fair dealing principles in contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309 requires that when a contract doesn't specify the time for shipment or other action, the time for the action must be a reasonable time. Reducing the notification period to 1 day might not be considered a reasonable time, particularly for complex or large orders.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the notice period for deferring or changing product shipments to 1 day. UCC § 2-309(1) states that if a contract does not specify the time for shipment, the time for the action must be a reasonable time. The law explanation suggests that 1 day may not be considered a reasonable time, particularly for complex or large orders. By reducing the notification period, the modified contract text potentially contradicts the requirement for a reasonable time frame as mandated by UCC § 2-309(1). This is an inconsistency that leads to a legal contradiction because a deadline has been introduced that violates state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 6.2 Upon and subject to credit approval by Cisco, payment terms shall be [*****] days from shipping date. All payments shall be made in the currency in which the Price List is quoted for the applicable Purchase Order [*****].
perturbation.changed_text: 6.2 Upon and subject to credit approval by Cisco, payment terms shall be 600 days from shipping date. All payments shall be made in the currency in which the Price List is quoted for the applicable Purchase Order [*****].
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to 600 days could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under contract law. Such an extended payment period is commercially unreasonable and places an undue burden on the seller.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 states that every contract or duty within the UCC imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Setting a payment term of 600 days is likely to be deemed commercially unreasonable and therefore in violation of the duty of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the payment terms from an unspecified number of days (represented by [*****]) to a fixed 600 days. This extended payment period is argued to violate the UCC's implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 1-304, states that "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that a 600-day payment term is likely commercially unreasonable and therefore violates this duty of good faith, creating a contradiction under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition.

Distributor shall make these records available for audit by Cisco upon [*****] prior written notice, during regular business hours, at Distributor's principal place of business or such other of Distributor's locations where Distributor may maintain relevant records. perturbation.changed_text: 17.1 With regard to both Distributor and Affiliates' activity, Distributor shall keep full, true, and accurate records and accounts, in accordance with generally-accepted accounting principles, of each Product or Service purchased and deployed, Resold, or distributed by serial number, including information regarding compliance with Cisco marketing and sales programs, Software usage, and export or transfer. Distributor shall make these records available for audit by Cisco upon 1 hour prior written notice, during regular business hours, at Distributor's principal place of business or such other of Distributor's locations where Distributor may maintain relevant records. perturbation.explanation: Changing the audit notice period to '1 hour' is likely to be deemed commercially unreasonable and may violate legal principles requiring reasonable notice for audits.
perturbation.law_explanation: Good faith is required in the performance and enforcement of every contract. Auditors would need significantly more than 1 hour to effectively prepare for an audit. This contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, particularly because audits usually require a large amount of time to gather all documents.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. perturbation.justification: The changed text, by reducing the audit notice period to 1 hour, contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." Providing only 1 hour of notice for an audit is commercially unreasonable, creating an inconsistency that violates this obligation. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight how such a short notice period is insufficient for preparing for an audit, essentially undermining the audit's purpose and effectiveness.
